params.text:
Promoting EDI in everyday care

Promoting equality, diversity and inclusion is not a one-off task – it is woven into the small, everyday choices you make. Inclusive care is another way of describing truly person-centred care: care shaped around the individual, respecting their dignity, human rights and right to make their own choices.

You promote EDI when you:

  • Get to know the whole person – their history, culture, faith, relationships and what matters most to them.
  • Offer real choice over food, clothing, routine, activities and how they wish to be addressed.
  • Respect cultural and religious needs – diet, prayer, festivals, dress and personal care preferences.
  • Communicate in a way that works for the person, adapting your approach and using aids where needed.
  • Support relationships and identity, including a person's partner, family, community and sexual orientation.
  • Challenge discrimination when you see it, rather than letting it pass.

These actions protect dignity and help every person feel valued for who they are.

params.title: Tap a card to flip it
params.description: Everyday situations in care. Flip each card for an inclusive, person-centred response.

dialogs.text: A resident observes a religious festival that involves fasting at certain times.
dialogs.answer: Learn about the festival, adjust mealtimes to suit them, and support them to observe it – respecting religion and belief.

dialogs.text: A person who is gay wants their partner involved in decisions about their care.
dialogs.answer: Welcome and involve the partner as you would any spouse or family member – respecting sexual orientation and relationships.

dialogs.text: Someone speaks little English and seems anxious during personal care.
dialogs.answer: Use an interpreter or translated materials, communicate patiently, and check consent – making a reasonable adjustment.

dialogs.text: A resident with dementia repeatedly asks to wear a particular scarf.
dialogs.answer: Honour the choice; clothing is part of identity and dignity. Offer choice rather than deciding for them.
